title: Today's Tech Brief: Do You Actually Need to Pay for Transcription Software?
summary: A look at the latest in transcription software and AI agents in the workplace.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Do You Actually Need to Pay for Transcription Software?
imageCaption: Do You Actually Need to Pay for Transcription Software?
imageSourceTitle: Do You Actually Need to Pay for Transcription Software?

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's tech brief. I'm always on the lookout for tools that can make my life easier, and I've been seeing a lot of ads for AI-powered transcription software like Wispr Flow. The idea is that you can talk out loud instead of typing, which sounds great, especially if you're a slow typist like me.

First up, an article from Wired asks, 'Do You Actually Need to Pay for Transcription Software?' The piece takes a closer look at Wispr Flow and other transcription tools, wondering if they're worth the cost. I'm curious to see if these tools can really deliver on their promise of making writing faster and easier.

Next, a story from MIT Technology Review caught my eye. It's about rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI. Apparently, there's a disconnect between companies' ambitions to use AI and their ability to actually make it work. I'm intrigued to see how companies will adapt to this new technology.

Also on the board, I'm looking at the potential impact of AI agents on the workplace. With 85% of organizations saying they want to be more 'agentic' with AI, it's clear that there's a lot of interest in this technology. But will it live up to the hype?

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
